title: Deformable DETR: Deformable Transformers for End-to-End Object Detection

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DEFORMABLE DETR: DEFORMABLE TRANSFORMERS - FOR END TO-END OBJECT DETECTION
**Xizhou Zhu**[1] _[∗]_ **, Weijie Su**[2] _[∗‡]_ **, Lewei Lu**[1] **, Bin Li**[2] **, Xiaogang Wang**[1] _[,]_[3] **, Jifeng Dai**[1] _[†]_

1SenseTime Research

2University of Science and Technology of China

3The Chinese University of Hong Kong
_{_ zhuwalter,luotto,daijifeng _}_ @sensetime.com jackroos@mail.ustc.edu.cn, binli@ustc.edu.cn xgwang@ee.cuhk.edu.hk

## ABSTRACT

DETR has been recently proposed to eliminate the need for many hand-designed components in object detection while demonstrating good performance. However, it suffers from slow convergence and limited feature spatial resolution, due to the limitation of Transformer attention modules in processing image feature maps. To mitigate these issues, we proposed Deformable DETR, whose attention modules only attend to a small set of key sampling points around a reference. Deformable DETR can achieve better performance than DETR (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. Extensive experiments on the COCO benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Code is released at https:// github.com/fundamentalvision/Deformable-DETR.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Modern object detectors employ many hand-crafted components (Liu et al., 2020), e.g., anchor generation, rule-based training target assignment, non-maximum suppression (NMS) post-processing. They are not fully end-to-end. Recently, Carion et al. (2020) proposed DETR to eliminate the need for such hand-crafted components, and built the first fully end-to-end object detector, achieving very competitive performance. DETR utilizes a simple architecture, by combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) encoder-decoders. They exploit the versatile and powerful relation modeling capability of Transformers to replace the hand-crafted rules, under properly designed training signals.

Despite its interesting design and good performance, DETR has its own issues: (1) It requires much longer training epochs to converge than the existing object detectors. For example, on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark, DETR needs 500 epochs to converge, which is around 10 to 20 times slower than Faster R-CNN (Ren et al., 2015). (2) DETR delivers relatively low performance at detecting small objects. Modern object detectors usually exploit multi-scale features, where small objects are detected from high-resolution feature maps. Meanwhile, high-resolution feature maps lead to unacceptable complexities for DETR. The above-mentioned issues can be mainly attributed to the deficit of Transformer components in processing image feature maps. At initialization, the attention modules cast nearly uniform attention weights to all the pixels in the feature maps. Long training epoches is necessary for the attention weights to be learned to focus on sparse meaningful locations. On the other hand, the attention weights computation in Transformer encoder is of quadratic computation w.r.t. pixel numbers. Thus, it is of very high computational and memory complexities to process high-resolution feature maps.

In the image domain, deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017) is of a powerful and efficient mechanism to attend to sparse spatial locations. It naturally avoids the above-mentioned issues. While it lacks the element relation modeling mechanism, which is the key for the success of DETR.

In this paper, we propose _Deformable DETR_ , which mitigates the slow convergence and high complexity issues of DETR. It combines the best of the sparse spatial sampling of deformable convolution, and the relation modeling capability of Transformers. We propose the _deformable attention module_ , which attends to a small set of sampling locations as a pre-filter for prominent key elements out of all the feature map pixels. The module can be naturally extended to aggregating multi-scale features, without the help of FPN (Lin et al., 2017a). In Deformable DETR , we utilize (multi-scale) deformable attention modules to replace the Transformer attention modules processing feature maps, as shown in Fig. 1.

Deformable DETR opens up possibilities for us to exploit variants of end-to-end object detectors, thanks to its fast convergence, and computational and memory efficiency. We explore a simple and effective _iterative bounding box refinement_ mechanism to improve the detection performance. We also try a _two-stage Deformable DETR_ , where the region proposals are also generated by a vaiant of Deformable DETR, which are further fed into the decoder for iterative bounding box refinement.

Extensive experiments on the COCO (Lin et al., 2014) benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Compared with DETR, Deformable DETR can achieve better performance (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. The proposed variant of two-stage Deformable DETR can further improve the performance. Code is released at https://github. com/fundamentalvision/Deformable-DETR.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Efficient Attention Mechanism.** Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) involve both self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms. One of the most well-known concern of Transformers is the high time and memory complexity at vast key element numbers, which hinders model scalability in many cases. Recently, many efforts have been made to address this problem (Tay et al., 2020b), which can be roughly divided into three categories in practice.

The first category is to use pre-defined sparse attention patterns on keys. The most straightforward paradigm is restricting the attention pattern to be fixed local windows. Most works (Liu et al., 2018a; Parmar et al., 2018; Child et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; Ho et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020a; Hu et al., 2019; Ramachandran et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2019; Beltagy et al., 2020; Ainslie et al., 2020; Zaheer et al., 2020) follow this paradigm. Although restricting the attention pattern to a local neighborhood can decrease the complexity, it loses global information. To compensate, Child et al. (2019); Huang et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2020a) attend key elements

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

at fixed intervals to significantly increase the receptive field on keys. Beltagy et al. (2020); Ainslie et al. (2020); Zaheer et al. (2020) allow a small number of special tokens having access to all key elements. Zaheer et al. (2020); Qiu et al. (2019) also add some pre-fixed sparse attention patterns to attend distant key elements directly.

The second category is to learn data-dependent sparse attention. Kitaev et al. (2020) proposes a locality sensitive hashing (LSH) based attention, which hashes both the query and key elements to different bins. A similar idea is proposed by Roy et al. (2020), where k-means finds out the most related keys. Tay et al. (2020a) learns block permutation for block-wise sparse attention.

The third category is to explore the low-rank property in self-attention. Wang et al. (2020b) reduces the number of key elements through a linear projection on the size dimension instead of the channel dimension. Katharopoulos et al. (2020); Choromanski et al. (2020) rewrite the calculation of selfattention through kernelization approximation.

In the image domain, the designs of efficient attention mechanism (e.g., Parmar et al. (2018); Child et al. (2019); Huang et al. (2019); Ho et al. (2019); Wang et al. (2020a); Hu et al. (2019); Ramachandran et al. (2019)) are still limited to the first category. Despite the theoretically reduced complexity, Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019) admit such approaches are much slower in implementation than traditional convolution with the same FLOPs (at least 3 _×_ slower), due to the intrinsic limitation in memory access patterns.

On the other hand, as discussed in Zhu et al. (2019a), there are variants of convolution, such as deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019b) and dynamic convolution (Wu et al., 2019), that also can be viewed as self-attention mechanisms. Especially, deformable convolution operates much more effectively and efficiently on image recognition than Transformer self-attention. Meanwhile, it lacks the element relation modeling mechanism.

Our proposed deformable attention module is inspired by deformable convolution, and belongs to the second category. It only focuses on a small fixed set of sampling points predicted from the feature of query elements. Different from Ramachandran et al. (2019); Hu et al. (2019), deformable attention is just slightly slower than the traditional convolution under the same FLOPs.

**Multi-scale Feature Representation for Object Detection.** One of the main difficulties in object detection is to effectively represent objects at vastly different scales. Modern object detectors usually exploit multi-scale features to accommodate this. As one of the pioneering works, FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) proposes a top-down path to combine multi-scale features. PANet (Liu et al., 2018b) further adds an bottom-up path on the top of FPN. Kong et al. (2018) combines features from all scales by a global attention operation. Zhao et al. (2019) proposes a U-shape module to fuse multi-scale features. Recently, NAS-FPN (Ghiasi et al., 2019) and Auto-FPN (Xu et al., 2019) are proposed to automatically design cross-scale connections via neural architecture search. Tan et al. (2020) proposes the BiFPN, which is a repeated simplified version of PANet. Our proposed multi-scale deformable attention module can naturally aggregate multi-scale feature maps via attention mechanism, without the help of these feature pyramid networks.

## 3 REVISITING TRANSFORMERS AND DETR

**Multi-Head Attention in Transformers.** Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017) are of a network architecture based on attention mechanisms for machine translation. Given a query element (e.g., a target word in the output sentence) and a set of key elements (e.g., source words in the input sentence), the _multi-head attention module_ adaptively aggregates the key contents according to the attention weights that measure the compatibility of query-key pairs. To allow the model focusing on contents from different representation subspaces and different positions, the outputs of different attention heads are linearly aggregated with learnable weights. Then the multi-head attention feature is calculated by

There are two known issues with Transformers. One is Transformers need long training schedules before convergence. Suppose the number of query and key elements are of _Nq_ and _Nk_ , respectively. Typically, with proper parameter initialization, _**U** m_ _**z** q_ and _**V** m_ _**x** k_ follow distribution with mean of 1 0 and variance of 1, which makes attention weights _Amqk ≈ Nk_[,][when] _[N][k]_[is][large.][It][will][lead] to ambiguous gradients for input features. Thus, long training schedules are required so that the attention weights can focus on specific keys. In the image domain, where the key elements are usually of image pixels, _Nk_ can be very large and the convergence is tedious.

On the other hand, the computational and memory complexity for multi-head attention can be very high with numerous query and key elements. The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of _O_ ( _NqC_[2] + _NkC_[2] + _NqNkC_ ). In the image domain, where the query and key elements are both of pixels, _Nq_ = _Nk ≫ C_ , the complexity is dominated by the third term, as _O_ ( _NqNkC_ ). Thus, the multi-head attention module suffers from a quadratic complexity growth with the feature map size.

**DETR.** DETR (Carion et al., 2020) is built upon the Transformer encoder-decoder architecture, combined with a set-based Hungarian loss that forces unique predictions for each ground-truth bounding box via bipartite matching. We briefly review the network architecture as follows.

Given the input feature maps _**x** ∈_ R _[C][×][H][×][W]_ extracted by a CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet (He et al., 2016)), DETR exploits a standard Transformer encoder-decoder architecture to transform the input feature maps to be features of a set of object queries. A 3-layer feed-forward neural network (FFN) and a linear projection are added on top of the object query features (produced by the decoder) as the detection head. The FFN acts as the regression branch to predict the bounding box coordinates _**b** ∈_ [0 _,_ 1][4] , where _**b**_ = _{bx, by, bw, bh}_ encodes the normalized box center coordinates, box height and width (relative to the image size). The linear projection acts as the classification branch to produce the classification results.

For the Transformer encoder in DETR, both query and key elements are of pixels in the feature maps. The inputs are of ResNet feature maps (with encoded positional embeddings). Let _H_ and _W_ denote the feature map height and width, respectively. The computational complexity of self-attention is of _O_ ( _H_[2] _W_[2] _C_ ), which grows quadratically with the spatial size.

For the Transformer decoder in DETR, the input includes both feature maps from the encoder, and _N_ object queries represented by learnable positional embeddings (e.g., _N_ = 100). There are two types of attention modules in the decoder, namely, cross-attention and self-attention modules. In the cross-attention modules, object queries extract features from the feature maps. The query elements are of the object queries, and key elements are of the output feature maps from the encoder. In it, _Nq_ = _N_ , _Nk_ = _H × W_ and the complexity of the cross-attention is of _O_ ( _HWC_[2] + _NHWC_ ). The complexity grows linearly with the spatial size of feature maps. In the self-attention modules, object queries interact with each other, so as to capture their relations. The query and key elements are both of the object queries. In it, _Nq_ = _Nk_ = _N_ , and the complexity of the self-attention module is of _O_ (2 _NC_[2] + _N_[2] _C_ ). The complexity is acceptable with moderate number of object queries.

DETR is an attractive design for object detection, which removes the need for many hand-designed components. However, it also has its own issues. These issues can be mainly attributed to the deficits of Transformer attention in handling image feature maps as key elements: (1) DETR has relatively low performance in detecting small objects. Modern object detectors use high-resolution feature maps to better detect small objects. However, high-resolution feature maps would lead to an unacceptable complexity for the self-attention module in the Transformer encoder of DETR, which has a quadratic complexity with the spatial size of input feature maps. (2) Compared with modern object detectors, DETR requires many more training epochs to converge. This is mainly because the attention modules processing image features are difficult to train. For example, at initialization, the cross-attention modules are almost of average attention on the whole feature maps. While, at the end of the training, the attention maps are learned to be very sparse, focusing only on the object

## 4 METHOD

## 4.1 DEFORMABLE TRANSFORMERS FOR END-TO-END OBJECT DETECTION

**Deformable Attention Module.** The core issue of applying Transformer attention on image feature maps is that it would look over all possible spatial locations. To address this, we present a _deformable attention module_ . Inspired by deformable convolution (Dai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019b), the deformable attention module only attends to a small set of key sampling points around a reference point, regardless of the spatial size of the feature maps, as shown in Fig. 2. By assigning only a small fixed number of keys for each query, the issues of convergence and feature spatial resolution can be mitigated.

**Deformable Transformer Encoder.** We replace the Transformer attention modules processing feature maps in DETR with the proposed multi-scale deformable attention module. Both the input and output of the encoder are of multi-scale feature maps with the same resolutions. In encoder, we extract multi-scale feature maps _{_ _**x**[l] }[L] l_ =1 _[−]_[1][(] _[L]_[ = 4][) from the output feature maps of stages] _[ C]_[3][ through] _C_ 5 in ResNet (He et al., 2016) (transformed by a 1 _×_ 1 convolution), where _Cl_ is of resolution 2 _[l]_ lower than the input image. The lowest resolution feature map _**x**[L]_ is obtained via a 3 _×_ 3 stride 2 convolution on the final _C_ 5 stage, denoted as _C_ 6. All the multi-scale feature maps are of _C_ = 256 channels. Note that the top-down structure in FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) is not used, because our proposed multi-scale deformable attention in itself can exchange information among multi-scale feature maps. The constructing of multi-scale feature maps are also illustrated in Appendix A.2. Experiments in Section 5.2 show that adding FPN will not improve the performance. In application of the multi-scale deformable attention module in encoder, the output are of multiscale feature maps with the same resolutions as the input. Both the key and query elements are of pixels from the multi-scale feature maps. For each query pixel, the reference point is itself. To identify which feature level each query pixel lies in, we add a scale-level embedding, denoted as _**e** l_ , to the feature representation, in addition to the positional embedding. Different from the positional embedding with fixed encodings, the scale-level embedding _{_ _**e** l}[L] l_ =1[are][randomly][initialized][and] jointly trained with the network.

**Deformable Transformer Decoder.** There are cross-attention and self-attention modules in the decoder. The query elements for both types of attention modules are of object queries. In the crossattention modules, object queries extract features from the feature maps, where the key elements are of the output feature maps from the encoder. In the self-attention modules, object queries interact with each other, where the key elements are of the object queries. Since our proposed deformable attention module is designed for processing convolutional feature maps as key elements, we only replace each cross-attention module to be the multi-scale deformable attention module, while leaving the self-attention modules unchanged. For each object query, the 2-d normalized coordinate of the

reference point _**p**_ ˆ _q_ is predicted from its object query embedding via a learnable linear projection followed by a sigmoid function.

Because the multi-scale deformable attention module extracts image features around the reference point, we let the detection head predict the bounding box as relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point to further reduce the optimization difficulty. The reference point is used as the initial guess of the box center. The detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point. Check Appendix A.3 for the details. In this way, the learned decoder attention will have strong correlation with the predicted bounding boxes, which also accelerates the training convergence.
By replacing Transformer attention modules with deformable attention modules in DETR, we establish an efficient and fast converging detection system, dubbed as Deformable DETR (see Fig. 1).

## 4.2 ADDITIONAL IMPROVEMENTS AND VARIANTS FOR DEFORMABLE DETR

Deformable DETR opens up possibilities for us to exploit various variants of end-to-end object detectors, thanks to its fast convergence, and computational and memory efficiency. Due to limited space, we only introduce the core ideas of these improvements and variants here. The implementation details are given in Appendix A.4.

**Iterative Bounding Box Refinement.** This is inspired by the iterative refinement developed in optical flow estimation (Teed & Deng, 2020). We establish a simple and effective iterative bounding box refinement mechanism to improve detection performance. Here, each decoder layer refines the bounding boxes based on the predictions from the previous layer.

**Two-Stage Deformable DETR.** In the original DETR, object queries in the decoder are irrelevant to the current image. Inspired by two-stage object detectors, we explore a variant of Deformable DETR for generating region proposals as the first stage. The generated region proposals will be fed into the decoder as object queries for further refinement, forming a two-stage Deformable DETR.

In the first stage, to achieve high-recall proposals, each pixel in the multi-scale feature maps would serve as an object query. However, directly setting object queries as pixels will bring unacceptable computational and memory cost for the self-attention modules in the decoder, whose complexity grows quadratically with the number of queries. To avoid this problem, we remove the decoder and form an encoder-only Deformable DETR for region proposal generation. In it, each pixel is assigned as an object query, which directly predicts a bounding box. Top scoring bounding boxes are picked as region proposals. No NMS is applied before feeding the region proposals to the second stage.

## 5 EXPERIMENT

**Dataset.** We conduct experiments on COCO 2017 dataset (Lin et al., 2014). Our models are trained on the train set, and evaluated on the val set and test-dev set.

**Implementation Details.** ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) pre-trained ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) is utilized as the backbone for ablations. Multi-scale feature maps are extracted without FPN (Lin et al., 2017a). _M_ = 8 and _K_ = 4 are set for deformable attentions by default. Parameters of the deformable Transformer encoder are shared among different feature levels. Other hyper-parameter setting and training strategy mainly follow DETR (Carion et al., 2020), except that Focal Loss (Lin et al., 2017b) with loss weight of 2 is used for bounding box classification, and the number of object queries is increased from 100 to 300. We also report the performance of DETR-DC5 with these modifications for a fair comparison, denoted as DETR-DC5[+] . By default, models are trained for 50 epochs and the learning rate is decayed at the 40-th epoch by a factor of 0.1. Following DETR(Carion et al., 2020), we train our models using Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with base learning rate of 2 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] , _β_ 1 = 0 _._ 9, _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 999, and weight decay of 10 _[−]_[4] . Learning rates of the linear projections, used for predicting object query reference points and sampling offsets, are multiplied by a factor of 0.1. Run time is evaluated on NVIDIA Tesla V100 GPU.

## 5.1 COMPARISON WITH DETR

As shown in Table 1, compared with Faster R-CNN + FPN, DETR requires many more training epochs to converge, and delivers lower performance at detecting small objects. Compared with

7

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

DETR, Deformable DETR achieves better performance (especially on small objects) with 10 _×_ less training epochs. Detailed convergence curves are shown in Fig. 3. With the aid of iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage paradigm, our method can further improve the detection accuracy.

Our proposed Deformable DETR has on par FLOPs with Faster R-CNN + FPN and DETR-DC5. But the runtime speed is much faster (1.6 _×_ ) than DETR-DC5, and is just 25% slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN. The speed issue of DETR-DC5 is mainly due to the large amount of memory access in Transformer attention. Our proposed deformable attention can mitigate this issue, at the cost of unordered memory access. Thus, it is still slightly slower than traditional convolution.

## 5.2 ABLATION STUDY ON DEFORMABLE ATTENTION

Table 2 presents ablations for various design choices of the proposed deformable attention module. Using multi-scale inputs instead of single-scale inputs can effectively improve detection accuracy with 1 _._ 7% AP, especially on small objects with 2 _._ 9% APS. Increasing the number of sampling points _K_ can further improve 0 _._ 9% AP. Using multi-scale deformable attention, which allows information exchange among different scale levels, can bring additional 1 _._ 5% improvement in AP. Because the cross-level feature exchange is already adopted, adding FPNs will not improve the performance. When multi-scale attention is not applied, and _K_ = 1, our (multi-scale) deformable attention module degenerates to deformable convolution, delivering noticeable lower accuracy.

## 5.3 COMPARISON WITH STATE-OF-THE-ART METHODS

Table 3 compares the proposed method with other state-of-the-art methods. Iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage mechanism are both utilized by our models in Table 3. With ResNet-101 and ResNeXt-101 (Xie et al., 2017), our method achieves 48.7 AP and 49.0 AP without bells and whistles, respectively. By using ResNeXt-101 with DCN (Zhu et al., 2019b), the accuracy rises to 50.1 AP. With additional test-time augmentations, the proposed method achieves 52.3 AP.

## 6 CONCLUSION

Deformable DETR is an end-to-end object detector, which is efficient and fast-converging. It enables us to explore more interesting and practical variants of end-to-end object detectors. At the core of Deformable DETR are the (multi-scale) deformable attention modules, which is an efficient attention mechanism in processing image feature maps. We hope our work opens up new possibilities in exploring end-to-end object detection.

coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3-flash-preview)
coarse__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Deformable DETR, which replaces the global dense attention of the original DETR with a sparse deformable attention mechanism. This change addresses the quadratic complexity of standard Transformers and the slow convergence of DETR, enabling the use of multi-scale feature maps for improved small object detection.

The paper makes a significant contribution by bridging deformable convolutions and Transformers, effectively solving the most critical bottlenecks of the DETR architecture. The results are impressive, showing a 10x speedup in convergence and substantial gains in mAP, particularly for small objects, while maintaining an end-to-end pipeline.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient analysis of sampling point hyperparameter K: In Section 4.1, the paper defines the deformable attention module using a fixed number of sampling points K. While Table 2 shows that K=4 outperforms K=1, there is no exploration of the upper bounds or diminishing returns for K. Since the core claim is that sparse sampling captures sufficient context, it is important to know at what point increasing K fails to provide further mAP gains or when the computational overhead begins to negate the efficiency benefits. Adding an ablation row for K=8 or K=16 would clarify if the model is sensitive to this sparsity constraint or if the current choice is a local optimum.

- Ambiguity in the stability of learned offsets: The mechanism relies on predicting offsets from query features to sample specific locations (Equation 2). However, the paper lacks a visualization or statistical analysis of how these offsets behave across different object scales or during the early versus late stages of training. If the offsets are unstable or fail to converge to meaningful object boundaries, the 'pre-filtering' logic described in Section 4.1 might be less effective than claimed. Providing a visualization of the sampling points for small versus large objects in the encoder and decoder would verify that the model actually learns to attend to relevant geometric structures.

- Computational complexity scaling with image resolution: Section 4.1 claims linear complexity with respect to spatial size, but the practical memory footprint of multi-scale deformable attention on very high-resolution images is not fully detailed. While the FLOPs are reported in Table 1, the peak memory usage during training is a common bottleneck for DETR-like models. A comparison of memory consumption (GB) across different input resolutions (e.g., 800px vs 1333px) would help practitioners understand the hardware requirements, especially since the multi-scale approach significantly increases the number of keys processed by the decoder.

- Interaction between iterative refinement and two-stage initialization: Section 4.2 introduces both iterative bounding box refinement and a two-stage variant, but it is unclear how much these two components overlap in their benefits. Table 1 shows they are additive, yet the paper does not discuss if the two-stage proposal generation makes the early layers of iterative refinement redundant. A more detailed breakdown of the error types (e.g., localization vs. classification) that each variant fixes would clarify whether the two-stage approach primarily helps with query initialization or if it provides a fundamentally different signal than the refinement layers.

- Lack of backbone independence verification: The majority of the results in Table 1 and Table 2 use ResNet-50. While Table 3 includes larger backbones, the paper assumes the performance gains from deformable attention are independent of the feature extractor's architecture. Given that deformable convolution (DCN) is often used in the backbone itself (as seen in the last rows of Table 3), there is a risk of confounding the benefits of deformable attention in the Transformer with the benefits of deformation in the CNN. An experiment using a non-DCN backbone versus a DCN backbone for the baseline DETR would isolate whether the Transformer-level changes are the sole driver of the observed improvements.

- Missing comparison to standard DETR with multi-scale features: The paper attributes DETR's low performance on small objects to the inability to use high-resolution feature maps due to quadratic complexity. However, it does not provide a baseline where the original DETR is trained with a multi-scale feature pyramid (e.g., using a restricted global attention or a smaller backbone) to isolate the benefits of the deformable mechanism from the benefits of multi-scale features. Without this, it is unclear if the gains in Table 1 come from the sparse sampling logic or simply from the fact that the model now sees higher-resolution inputs that the original DETR could not ingest. A necessary addition is a version of DETR-DC5 modified to accept the same multi-scale inputs as Deformable DETR, perhaps by using a simplified global attention, to verify that the deformable attention is the superior way to handle these scales.

- Incomplete implementation guidance for the two-stage proposal generation: The two-stage variant is a major contribution, yet the paper lacks a discussion on how the model handles the transition from encoder-only proposals to decoder queries. Specifically, it does not specify how the 'top scoring bounding boxes' are converted into the initial query embeddings for the decoder. Since these queries are now image-dependent rather than learnable embeddings, the paper should detail whether the query features are pooled from the feature map (like ROI Pooling) or if they are simply the projected coordinates. Adding a formal description of the mapping from the encoder's proposal output to the decoder's query input is required for the method to be reproducible.

- Missing analysis of the 'uniform attention' initialization claim: A central motivation for the work is that standard Transformers suffer from uniform attention at initialization, which slows convergence. While the paper proposes deformable attention as a solution, it does not provide an empirical measure of the 'sparsity' of attention weights over time to prove this hypothesis. The paper should include a plot comparing the entropy of attention weights in standard DETR versus the entropy of the sampling weights in Deformable DETR across the first 10 epochs. This would demonstrate that the deformable mechanism actually bypasses the 'diffuse gradient' problem mentioned in Section 3.

- Lack of failure case analysis for sparse sampling: The paper assumes that K=4 sampling points are sufficient to capture the context needed for detection. However, for very large or heavily occluded objects, 4 points might be insufficient to capture the global relationship modeling that DETR is known for. The paper lacks a qualitative or quantitative comparison of failure modes where the sparse sampling fails but global attention succeeds. A comparison of mAP for 'Large' objects (AP_L) in Table 1 shows that Deformable DETR (58.0) actually performs worse than the original DETR (61.1). The paper needs to acknowledge and analyze this performance drop to provide a complete picture of the trade-offs involved in sparse sampling.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include an ablation study for the number of sampling points K beyond the value of 4 to identify the saturation point.

- Provide visualizations of sampling point locations for objects of varying scales to verify geometric learning.

- Report peak GPU memory usage during training for different input resolutions to substantiate efficiency claims.

- Analyze the specific error reductions (localization vs. classification) provided by the two-stage and iterative refinement modules.

comments.title: Inaccurate Equivalence Claim to Transformer Attention
comments.quote: When the sampling points traverse all possible locations, the proposed attention module is equivalent to Transformer attention.
comments.explanation: This claim is mathematically incorrect because it overlooks the content-content term of standard Transformer attention. In standard Transformers, the attention weight is a function of the compatibility between a query and a key, meaning the weights are data-dependent on the features at the target locations. In Deformable Attention, the weights and offsets are predicted solely from the query feature via linear projections. Even if the number of sampling points covers all pixels, the mechanism remains a query-dependent spatial filter rather than a full pairwise relationship between query and key features. The text should state that it becomes equivalent to a query-only or content-independent attention mechanism.

comments.title: Inconsistency in query definition for the two-stage variant
comments.quote: In the first stage, to achieve high-recall proposals, each pixel in the multi-scale feature maps would serve as an object query. However, directly setting object queries as pixels will bring unacceptable computational and memory cost for the self-attention modules in the decoder, whose complexity grows quadratically with the number of queries.
comments.explanation: The text claims that using every pixel as an object query in the decoder is computationally prohibitive due to quadratic complexity. However, the core contribution of this paper is the Deformable Attention module, which is explicitly designed to have linear complexity with respect to the number of keys. If the decoder uses Deformable Attention, the complexity should be linear, not quadratic. This creates a logical contradiction: either the decoder is not using Deformable Attention for its self-attention component, or the justification for an encoder-only stage ignores the paper's primary innovation. The justification needs to clarify that the quadratic bottleneck arises specifically from the query-to-query self-attention in the decoder, which remains dense.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reference point prediction for decoder queries
comments.quote:
For each object query, the 2-d normalized coordinate of the

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

reference point $\hat{\bm{p}}_{q}$ is predicted from its object query embedding via a learnable linear projection followed by a sigmoid function.

Be
comments.explanation: In the standard DETR framework, object queries are learnable positional embeddings that are constant across the dataset. If the reference point is a direct linear projection of a static embedding, the reference points for each query slot would be fixed at the same spatial coordinates for every image regardless of content. This contradicts the goal of deformable sampling which should adapt to image content. The reference point should be image-dependent or the paper should explain how a static reference point per query slot is sufficient for the mechanism to function across diverse layouts.

comments.title: Ambiguity in proposal-to-query transformation
comments.quote: The generated region proposals will be fed into the decoder as object queries for further refinement, forming a two-stage Deformable DETR.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify the transformation from a geometric bounding box to the high-dimensional embedding required for a decoder query. In standard DETR, queries are learned positional embeddings; here, they must be derived from the first-stage encoder features at the proposal locations. Without defining whether this involves RoI pooling, sampling at the box center, or a specific projection of the encoder's hidden states, the two-stage mechanism is not reproducible. The performance of the second stage depends entirely on how the query features are initialized from the proposals.

comments.title: Inconsistency in AP_L performance claims
comments.quote: ach. Compared with DETR, Deformable DETR can achieve better performance (especially on small objects) with  $10 \times$  less training epoc
comments.explanation: The claim of better performance relative to DETR is not supported by Table 1 for all object scales. The baseline Deformable DETR at 50 epochs achieves 58.0 AP_L, which is lower than the 61.1 AP_L achieved by both DETR and DETR-DC5 at 500 epochs. While the model excels at small objects, the sparse sampling mechanism appears to introduce a performance trade-off for large objects that is not acknowledged. The text should specify that the performance improvement is concentrated on small and medium objects, while large object detection requires the iterative refinement module to reach parity.

comments.title: Incomplete complexity term for DETR Cross-Attention
comments.quote: n it, $N_{q}=N$, $N_{k}=H\times W$ and the complexity of the cross-attention is of $O(HWC^{2}+NHWC)$. The c
comments.explanation: The complexity for the cross-attention module is stated as O(HWC^2 + NHWC), but this omits the projection costs for the N object queries. Following the general formula provided in the previous paragraph, O(N_q C^2 + N_k C^2 + N_q N_k C), and substituting N_q=N and N_k=HW, the result should be O(NC^2 + HWC^2 + NHWC). While HW is much larger than N in standard DETR, the NC^2 term is required for mathematical consistency with the paper's own definitions.

comments.title: Logical gap in iterative refinement dependencies
comments.quote: Here, each decoder layer refines the bounding boxes based on the predictions from the previous layer.
comments.explanation: The description of iterative refinement is missing a detail regarding coordinate systems. If layer d refines the prediction of layer d-1, the reference points for the Deformable Attention in layer d must be updated to the output of layer d-1 to maintain consistency. If the reference points remain static, the refinement is merely a residual prediction on the same sampling locations. The text should explicitly state that the reference points are updated to the predicted box centers of the previous layer.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Deformable DETR, which modifies the transformer attention mechanism in DETR to use deformable sampling points. The approach shows significant improvements in training efficiency and small object detection performance. While the core idea is sound and the results are impressive, there are several methodological gaps that need addressing, particularly regarding the generalization of deformable attention across different object scales, the adequacy of ablation studies, and computational efficiency claims.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by successfully addressing two major limitations of DETR - slow convergence and poor small object performance. The deformable attention mechanism is an elegant solution that combines the benefits of deformable convolutions with transformer relation modeling. The experimental results on COCO are strong, showing 10x faster convergence and significant improvements in small object detection. The code release is also commendable for reproducibility.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient validation of small object performance claims: The paper claims that Deformable DETR particularly improves small object detection, showing AP_S improvements from 16.3% to 26.4% in Table 1. However, there is no systematic analysis of why deformable attention helps with small objects specifically. The ablation study in Table 2 shows multi-scale inputs contribute +2.9% AP_S, but there's no investigation into how the deformable sampling points behave for small versus large objects. The paper should include visualizations of attention maps for small objects and analyze whether the sampling points effectively focus on the limited pixel regions available for tiny objects. Without this analysis, the claim that the method 'especially' helps small objects remains empirically weak.

- Incomplete ablation of multi-scale feature fusion: Section 5.2's ablation study is insufficient to validate the claim that multi-scale deformable attention eliminates the need for FPN. The study only tests adding FPN or BiFPN on top of the full model, but doesn't compare against using FPN without multi-scale attention. A more rigorous ablation would include: (1) single-scale attention + FPN, (2) multi-scale attention without cross-level exchange, and (3) multi-scale attention with FPN. The current design doesn't isolate whether the performance comes from multi-scale inputs, cross-level attention, or both. This is critical given the paper's strong claim that 'adding FPN will not improve the performance' (Section 8).

- Unverified computational efficiency claims: The paper claims deformable attention is 'just slightly slower than traditional convolution' (Section 6.1) and shows 19 FPS in Table 1. However, there's no detailed analysis of memory access patterns or actual runtime breakdown. The complexity analysis in Appendix A.1 is mentioned but not shown in the provided content. Given that deformable attention involves unordered memory access due to dynamic sampling points, the paper should provide: (1) actual FLOPs versus runtime comparison, (2) memory consumption analysis, and (3) discussion of hardware-specific optimizations. Without this, the efficiency claims cannot be properly evaluated, especially for real-time applications.

- Lack of reference point initialization analysis: The decoder uses reference points predicted from object queries via a linear projection and sigmoid (Section 9). This is a critical design choice that affects convergence, yet the paper provides no analysis of how these reference points are initialized or how they evolve during training. The literature context specifically flags this as an assumption red flag. The paper should include: (1) analysis of reference point distribution at initialization, (2) visualization of how reference points change during training, and (3) ablation comparing different initialization strategies (e.g., uniform grid vs. learned). This is essential for understanding the fast convergence claim.

- Missing generalization analysis across datasets: All experiments are conducted solely on COCO dataset. While COCO is standard, the paper makes no attempt to validate whether the deformable attention mechanism generalizes to other domains with different object characteristics (e.g., aerial imagery, medical images). The literature context notes that generalization to unseen domains is an open question. The paper should include at least one additional dataset (e.g., Objects365 or LVIS) to demonstrate that the 10x convergence improvement and small object gains are not COCO-specific artifacts. This is particularly important given the method's reliance on learned sampling patterns.

- Missing visualization of deformable sampling behavior: The paper claims deformable attention improves small object detection but provides no visual evidence of how the sampling points actually behave. Readers need to see attention maps showing where deformable attention focuses for different object scales, especially tiny objects. The authors should include visualizations comparing standard DETR attention maps with Deformable DETR's sampling points on the same images, specifically highlighting cases with small objects. This would substantiate the claim that the method 'especially' helps small objects by showing whether the sparse sampling effectively concentrates on limited pixel regions.

- Incomplete FPN elimination validation: The paper claims multi-scale deformable attention eliminates the need for FPN but the ablation study in Table 2 doesn't properly test this. A complete validation requires comparing: (1) single-scale attention + FPN, (2) multi-scale attention without cross-level exchange, and (3) the proposed multi-scale attention with cross-level exchange. The current design only adds FPN on top of the full model, making it impossible to isolate whether performance gains come from multi-scale inputs or the cross-level attention mechanism. Without this systematic comparison, the claim that 'adding FPN will not improve the performance' remains unsubstantiated.

- Missing computational efficiency breakdown: The paper claims deformable attention is nearly as fast as convolution but provides no detailed runtime analysis. The authors should include: (1) actual FLOPs versus runtime measurements comparing deformable attention with standard attention and convolution, (2) memory consumption analysis during training and inference, and (3) discussion of how unordered memory access from dynamic sampling affects real-world performance. This breakdown is necessary to validate the efficiency claims, especially since the method targets practical applications where inference speed matters.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive analysis of deformable attention behavior on small objects, including attention map visualizations and quantitative analysis of sampling point distributions

- Expand ablation study to properly isolate contributions of multi-scale inputs versus cross-level attention, including comparison with FPN-based multi-scale fusion

- Provide detailed computational analysis including actual runtime measurements, memory consumption, and discussion of hardware optimization strategies

- Analyze reference point initialization and evolution during training, with ablation studies on different initialization strategies

- Include experiments on at least one additional dataset beyond COCO to validate generalization claims

comments.title: Dimensional error in attention weight formula
comments.quote: The attention weights $A_{mqk}\propto\exp\{\frac{\bm{z}_{q}^{\top}\bm{U}_{m}^{\top}\bm{V}_{m}\bm{x}_{k}}{\sqrt{C_{v}}}\}$
comments.explanation: The attention weight formula has a dimensional error. Given $\bm{z}_{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$, $\bm{U}_{m} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_{v}\times C}$, $\bm{V}_{m} \in \mathbb{R}^{C_{v}\times C}$, and $\bm{x}_{k} \in \mathbb{R}^{C}$, the expression $\bm{z}_{q}^{\top}\bm{U}_{m}^{\top}\bm{V}_{m}\bm{x}_{k}$ evaluates to $(1 \times C) \times (C \times C_{v}) \times (C_{v} \times C) \times (C \times 1) = 1 \times C_{v} \times C \times 1$, which is not a scalar. The correct formulation should be $A_{mqk} \propto \exp\{\frac{(\bm{U}_{m}\bm{z}_{q})^{\top}(\bm{V}_{m}\bm{x}_{k})}{\sqrt{C_{v}}}\}$ because this yields a scalar value suitable for attention weights.

comments.title: Incorrect computational complexity analysis
comments.quote: The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of $O(N_{q}C^{2}+N_{k}C^{2}+N_{q}N_{k}C)$. In the image domain, where the query and key elements are both of pixels, $N_{q}=N_{k}\gg C$, the complexity is dominated by the third term, as $O(N_{q}N_{k}C)$.
comments.explanation: The computational complexity analysis is incorrect. For multi-head attention with $M$ heads where each head processes $C_{v}=C/M$ dimensions, the projection operations cost $O(N_{q}CC_{v}+N_{k}CC_{v})$ not $O(N_{q}C^{2}+N_{k}C^{2})$. The attention computation costs $O(N_{q}N_{k}C_{v})$ per head, totaling $O(MN_{q}N_{k}C_{v}) = O(N_{q}N_{k}C)$. However, the first two terms are incorrect. Rewrite the quoted text as "The computational complexity of Eq. 1 is of $O(N_{q}CC_{v}+N_{k}CC_{v}+N_{q}N_{k}C)$. In the image domain, where the query and key elements are both of pixels, $N_{q}=N_{k}\gg C$, the complexity is dominated by the third term, as $O(N_{q}N_{k}C)$" because the projection terms should use $C_{v}$ not $C$.

comments.title: Decoder complexity analysis error
comments.quote: in DETR decoder, where $N_{q}=N$ ($N$ is the number of object queries), the complexity becomes $O(NKC^{2})$, which is irrelevant to the spatial size $HW$.
comments.explanation: Substituting $N_{q} = N$ into the general formula $O(2N_{q}C^{2} + \min (HWC^{2},N_{q}KC^{2}))$ gives $O(2NC^{2} + \min (HWC^{2},NKC^{2}))$. The paper incorrectly simplifies this to $O(NKC^{2})$ by dropping the $2NC^{2}$ term and assuming the min term always equals $NKC^{2}$. The correct complexity depends on the relative sizes of $HWC^{2}$ and $NKC^{2}$. When $NKC^{2} \geq HWC^{2}$, the complexity is $O(2NC^{2} + HWC^{2})$, which still depends on spatial size. Rewrite the decoder complexity as: "When it is applied as the cross-attention modules in DETR decoder, where $N_{q}=N$, the complexity becomes $O(2NC^{2} + \min (HWC^{2},NKC^{2}))$. When $NKC^{2} \geq HWC^{2}$, this simplifies to $O(2NC^{2} + HWC^{2})$, which still depends on spatial size."

comments.title: Incomplete deformable convolution degeneracy condition
comments.quote: The proposed attention module will degenerate to deformable convolution *(Dai et al., 2017)*, when $L=1$, $K=1$, and $\bm{W}_{m}^{\prime}\in\mathbb{R}^{C_{v}\times C}$ is fixed as an identity matrix.
comments.explanation: The degeneracy condition is incomplete. With $L=1$ and $K=1$, Equation 3 becomes $\sum_{m=1}^{M}\bm{W}_{m}[A_{m1q1} \cdot \bm{W}_{m}^{\prime}\bm{x}^{1}(\phi_{1}(\hat{\bm{p}}_{q})+\Delta\bm{p}_{m1q1})]$. For true equivalence to deformable convolution, we need: (1) $M=1$ to eliminate the head summation, (2) $A_{m1q1}=1$ for all queries to remove attention weighting, and (3) $\bm{W}_{m}^{\prime}=I$ as stated. Add 'and $M=1$ with $A_{m1q1}=1$ for all queries' after the existing conditions.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated FPN performance claim
comments.quote: Because the cross-level feature exchange is already adopted, adding FPNs will not improve the performance.
comments.explanation: The claim that adding FPNs will not improve performance due to cross-level feature exchange is not supported by the ablation study. The text only mentions testing multi-scale inputs and multi-scale deformable attention, but provides no experimental evidence comparing the model with FPN versus without FPN when cross-level exchange is present. To validate this claim, the authors need to include experimental results showing that adding FPN on top of the multi-scale deformable attention model yields no performance gain. Without this comparison, the statement remains an unsupported assertion.

comments.title: Missing single-scale FPN baseline in ablation
comments.quote: Table 2: Ablations for deformable attention on COCO 2017 val set. "MS inputs" indicates using multi-scale inputs. "MS attention" indicates using multi-scale deformable attention.  $K$  is the number of sampling points for each attention head on each feature level.
comments.explanation: The ablation study lacks a critical baseline: single-scale attention with FPN. The table includes multi-scale attention with FPN (first row) and multi-scale attention without FPN (last row), but no single-scale attention with FPN. This makes it impossible to determine whether the performance gains come from multi-scale inputs, multi-scale attention, or FPN itself. Without this baseline, the claim that multi-scale deformable attention eliminates the need for FPN cannot be properly validated. Add a row for single-scale inputs + single-scale attention + FPN to isolate FPN's contribution.

comments.title: Inconsistent FPS comparison in Table 1
comments.quote: Our proposed Deformable DETR has on par FLOPs with Faster R-CNN + FPN and DETR-DC5. But the runtime speed is much faster  $(1.6\times)$  than DETR-DC5, and is just  $25\%$  slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN.
comments.explanation: The text claims Deformable DETR is 25% slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN, but Table 1 shows 19 FPS vs 26 FPS respectively. 19/26 = 0.73, meaning Deformable DETR is actually 27% slower (or 73% of the speed), not 25% slower. The 1.6× speedup over DETR-DC5 is correct (19/12 = 1.58 ≈ 1.6), but the 25% figure is mathematically inaccurate. Rewrite the sentence as 'But the runtime speed is much faster (1.6×) than DETR-DC5, and is 27% slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN' to match the actual FPS values in the table.

comments.title: Function notation inconsistency
comments.quote: Function $\phi_{l}(\bm{p}_{q})$ in Equation 3 re-scales the normalized coordinates $\hat{\bm{p}}_{q}$ to the input feature map of the $l$-th level.
comments.explanation: There is a notation inconsistency. The text defines $\phi_{l}(\bm{p}_{q})$ but Equation 3 uses $\phi_{l}(\hat{\bm{p}}_{q})$. Since the function operates on normalized coordinates, it should be consistently defined as $\phi_{l}(\hat{\bm{p}}_{q})$. Rewrite 'Function $\phi_{l}(\bm{p}_{q})$ in Equation 3 re-scales the normalized coordinates $\hat{\bm{p}}_{q}$' as 'Function $\phi_{l}(\hat{\bm{p}}_{q})$ in Equation 3 re-scales the normalized coordinates $\hat{\bm{p}}_{q}$' to maintain consistency.

comments.title: Redundant sentence about detection head
comments.quote: Because the multi-scale deformable attention module extracts image features around the reference point, we let the detection head predict the bounding box as relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point to further reduce the optimization difficulty. The reference point is used as the initial guess of the box center. The detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point.
comments.explanation: The passage contains a redundant sentence that repeats the same information. The first and third sentences both state that 'the detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point.' This repetition creates confusion about whether these are describing different aspects of the same process or if there's an error in the description. Remove the third sentence 'The detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point.' because it duplicates the information already stated in the first sentence.

comments.title: Typographical error in grant number
comments.quote: the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grand No.U19B2044 and No.61836011.
comments.explanation: There is a typographical error in the acknowledgments section. The word 'grand' should be 'grant' as this refers to grant numbers from the funding agency. This appears to be a simple spelling error that should be corrected for proper attribution.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a well-motivated and impactful improvement over DETR by introducing deformable attention, addressing key limitations in convergence speed and small-object detection. The core idea is elegant, leveraging sparse sampling inspired by deformable convolutions while preserving Transformer-style relation modeling. The empirical results are strong, showing significant gains in AP and training efficiency on COCO, which has made this work highly influential in the object detection community.

Overview issues:

- Reference point initialization and update mechanism lacks justification: The model relies on learned reference points for object queries, which are predicted from query embeddings via a linear projection and sigmoid. However, the paper does not explain how these reference points are initialized or whether they are fixed or updated during decoding. Poor initialization could lead to sampling in irrelevant image regions, especially early in training. Given that deformable attention only samples around these references, their quality directly affects gradient flow and convergence. The paper should clarify the initialization scheme and provide ablation or visualization showing that reference points align with object locations as training progresses.

- Fixed number of sampling points limits adaptability to object scale and aspect ratio: The model uses a fixed $K=4$ sampling points per attention head across all queries and scales. This uniform sparsity may be suboptimal: small or elongated objects likely require denser or anisotropic sampling patterns. The assumption that four points suffice for all object geometries is not validated. The paper would benefit from either adaptive sampling (e.g., learnable $K$ per query) or at least an ablation showing performance sensitivity to $K$. Without this, the design appears heuristic rather than principled, especially given known challenges with extreme aspect ratios in detection.

- Multi-scale attention mechanism lacks scale-level selection analysis: While the multi-scale deformable attention samples across $L=4$ feature levels, the paper does not analyze how attention weights $A_{mlqk}$ distribute across scales. Are low-level features used for small objects? Is there a consistent pattern? Without such analysis, it's unclear whether the model meaningfully leverages multi-scale information or defaults to a single dominant level. A histogram or per-object-scale attention distribution would strengthen the claim that the mechanism adaptively fuses scales, particularly for $AP_S$ improvements.

- Decoder self-attention bottleneck not addressed despite encoder efficiency gains: The deformable attention replaces only cross-attention in the decoder, leaving self-attention among object queries unchanged. With $N=300$ queries, the self-attention complexity is $O(N^2C)$, which becomes a bottleneck as other components are optimized. The paper claims computational efficiency but does not justify why self-attention remains unmodified. Given that queries are sparse and semantically diverse, a sparse or low-rank variant could further improve speed. This omission undermines the end-to-end efficiency narrative, especially for real-time applications.

- Two-stage variant introduces inconsistency in training and inference: The two-stage Deformable DETR uses an encoder-only first stage to generate region proposals, removing the decoder entirely. However, this means the first stage is trained without the refinement mechanism present in inference. During inference, proposals are refined by the decoder, but during training, no such feedback exists. This train-inference mismatch may degrade performance. A more coherent design would retain a lightweight decoder in training or use knowledge distillation. The current formulation risks overfitting to proposal quality that won't be available at test time.

- Lack of failure analysis or limitations discussion: The paper emphasizes improvements but does not discuss failure modes. For example, are there cases where deformable attention fails to sample relevant regions? How does performance degrade on highly occluded or tiny objects? Without such analysis, the method's robustness is unclear. Given the sparse sampling assumption, one would expect degradation when sampling points miss object content. A qualitative analysis (e.g., attention heatmap examples) or ablation on object difficulty would strengthen the paper’s credibility and help future work address remaining gaps.

- No ablation on reference point initialization scheme: The model predicts reference points from object query embeddings via a linear projection and sigmoid, but the paper does not specify how these are initialized or whether they are learned. Since deformable attention samples around these points, poor initialization could lead to ineffective gradients early in training, undermining the claimed fast convergence. Readers will wonder whether the improvement stems from better optimization or simply a favorable initialization. The paper should include an ablation comparing different initialization strategies—e.g., uniform vs. grid-based vs. perturbed ground-truth centers—and report AP and convergence speed to validate the design choice.

- Missing analysis of sampling point distribution relative to objects: The deformable attention module samples $K=4$ points per head, but there is no evidence that these points actually land on or near object regions during training. Without this, the claim that the module focuses on 'prominent key elements' is unsubstantiated. A natural question is whether the learned offsets concentrate around object boundaries or textures, or if they drift randomly. The authors should visualize the spatial distribution of sampling points relative to ground-truth boxes in early vs. late epochs, or compute the percentage of sampling points falling within object masks. This would demonstrate that the mechanism learns meaningful spatial priors.

- No comparison to standard DETR attention maps: The paper argues that deformable attention accelerates convergence by avoiding uniform attention at initialization, but it never shows side-by-side attention maps from DETR and Deformable DETR at early epochs. This comparison is standard in attention mechanism papers and would directly support the core claim. For a few representative queries, the authors should plot attention weights in DETR (dense and diffuse) versus sampling locations in Deformable DETR (sparse and focused) at epoch 5 or 10. This would make the convergence advantage tangible and rule out alternative explanations.

- Efficiency claims lack per-component runtime breakdown: The paper reports overall inference speed and FLOPs but does not break down where time is spent across encoder, decoder cross-attention, and decoder self-attention. Given that self-attention among 300 queries remains quadratic, it may dominate runtime despite deformable attention gains. Readers will question whether the speedup is due to the proposed module or other factors like fewer epochs or smaller feature maps. The authors should provide a detailed timing profile—e.g., milliseconds per decoder layer—showing that deformable cross-attention is indeed faster than standard Transformer attention under the same setup.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Clarify the initialization and update mechanism for reference points, including whether they are static or dynamic across decoder layers.

- Provide analysis of multi-scale attention weight distribution across feature levels, particularly in relation to object scale.

- Include a failure mode or limitation analysis with visual examples showing when sampling points miss object content.

- Justify the fixed number of sampling points (K=4) with ablation or propose an adaptive alternative.

- Address the train-inference inconsistency in the two-stage variant by modifying the training scheme or decoder usage.

comments.title: Incorrect normalization of multi-scale attention weights
comments.quote: The scalar attention weight $A_{mlqk}$ is normalized by $\sum_{l=1}^{L}\sum_{k=1}^{K}A_{mlqk}=1$.
comments.explanation: The normalization $\sum_{l=1}^{L}\sum_{k=1}^{K}A_{mlqk}=1$ applies globally across all levels and sampling points for each head and query. This strong constraint risks suppressing useful contributions from certain scales if one level dominates the weights. A more flexible design would normalize attention independently per feature level—i.e., $\sum_{k=1}^{K} A_{mlqk} = 1$ for each $l$—to allow adaptive fusion based on scale-specific relevance. The current formulation may hinder the model’s ability to balance low- and high-resolution cues, particularly for small objects. The paper should clarify whether this global softmax is intentional or revise the normalization to support level-wise adaptability.

comments.title: Ambiguous re-scaling function $\phi_l$ lacks explicit definition
comments.quote: Function $\phi_{l}(\bm{p}_{q})$ in Equation 3 re-scales the normalized coordinates $\hat{\bm{p}}_{q}$ to the input feature map of the $l$-th level.
comments.explanation: The function $\phi_l$ is used in Equation (3) but never defined—neither its mathematical form nor implementation details are given. Since $\hat{\bm{p}}_q \in [0,1]^2$ and feature maps have different resolutions, a standard definition would be $\phi_l(\hat{\bm{p}}_q) = \hat{\bm{p}}_q \odot [W_l, H_l]$, but this is not stated. Without this, it is unclear whether offsets $\Delta\bm{p}_{mlqk}$ are in pixel space and compatible with the feature map grid. This ambiguity affects reproducibility and correctness of sampling. The authors should explicitly define $\phi_l(\hat{\bm{p}}_q) = \hat{\bm{p}}_q \cdot [W_l, H_l]^\top$ to resolve dimensional consistency.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of equivalence to Transformer attention
comments.quote: When the sampling points traverse all possible locations, the proposed attention module is equivalent to Transformer attention.
comments.explanation: This claim is incorrect. Standard Transformer attention computes attention weights via softmax over query-key dot products across all positions. In contrast, this module uses learned offsets and attention weights predicted directly from the query, without computing full key-query similarities. Even with dense sampling, the mechanism lacks global context modeling and dynamic weight computation based on key content. The two are functionally distinct: one is content-based global attention, the other is query-driven sparse sampling. The sentence should be removed or revised to state that the module can only approximate full attention under strong additional assumptions—such as full key access and similarity-based weights—which are not part of the current design.

comments.title: Reference point reuse lacks dynamic update mechanism
comments.quote:
For each object query, the 2-d normalized coordinate of the

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

reference point $\hat{\bm{p}}_{q}$ is predicted from its object query embedding via a learnable linear projection followed by a sigmoid function.

Because the multi-scale deformable attention module extracts image features around the reference point, we let the detection head predict the bounding box as relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point to further reduce the optimization difficulty. The reference point is used as the initial guess of the box center. The detection head predicts the relative offsets w.r.t. the reference point.
comments.explanation: The same reference point $\hat{\bm{p}}_q$ is used for both attention sampling and box regression, but the text does not clarify whether it is updated across decoder layers. If fixed, later stages cannot refine spatial focus based on attended features—undermining iterative refinement. If updated, the mechanism is not described. The current wording suggests $\hat{\bm{p}}_q$ is predicted only from the initial query embedding, implying it is static. This creates a contradiction: the benefit of alignment between attention and regression relies on dynamic adaptation. The authors should distinguish between initial $\hat{\bm{p}}_q^{(0)}$ and any layer-wise updated version $\hat{\bm{p}}_q^{(l)}$, and explicitly state whether updates occur and how.

comments.title: Circular logic in convergence acceleration claim
comments.quote: In this way, the learned decoder attention will have strong correlation with the predicted bounding boxes, which also accelerates the training convergence.
comments.explanation: This argument assumes convergence to explain convergence. The claim presumes that early attention sampling aligns with accurate box predictions, but if reference points are poorly initialized or sampling misses objects, early features will be irrelevant—leading to poor regression. The mechanism lacks an explanation for how it escapes this cold-start problem. The paper must either show that initial reference points are spatially coherent (e.g., via visualization or ablation) or introduce auxiliary mechanisms (e.g., auxiliary loss on reference points) to ensure meaningful early sampling. Otherwise, the convergence claim rests on circular reasoning.

comments.title: Decoder removal creates train-inference mismatch
comments.quote: To avoid this problem, we remove the decoder and form an encoder-only Deformable DETR for region proposal generation. In it, each pixel is assigned as an object query, which directly predicts a bounding box. Top scoring bounding boxes are picked as region proposals. No NMS is applied before feeding the region proposals to the second stage.
comments.explanation: The two-stage variant trains the first stage to produce final-quality boxes without decoder refinement, yet at inference these proposals are refined by the decoder. This creates a train-inference mismatch: the first stage learns without feedback about how its outputs will be adjusted downstream. It may over-correct or under-predict, leading to suboptimal interaction with the decoder. A more coherent design would either include a lightweight decoder during training or use knowledge distillation to align proposal outputs with refined outputs. The current setup risks degrading end-to-end consistency.

comments.title: Pixel-level queries imply infeasible computational cost
comments.quote: each pixel in the multi-scale feature maps would serve as an object query
comments.explanation: Treating every pixel as a query implies tens of thousands of queries (e.g., 100×100×4 scales = 40,000), and even without decoder self-attention, the encoder self-attention has $O(N^2)$ complexity—leading to ~1.6 billion attention pairs. This is computationally infeasible without architectural changes (e.g., local attention, striding, or grouping). The paper does not explain how this is resolved. The claim is not credible without clarification. The authors should specify the actual mechanism that makes pixel-level queries tractable—such as using only a subset of pixels or applying sparse attention—or revise the claim to reflect a downsampled set of locations.

comments.title: Incorrect claim of degeneracy to deformable convolution
comments.quote: When multi-scale attention is not applied, and  $K = 1$ , our (multi-scale) deformable attention module degenerates to deformable convolution, delivering noticeable lower accuracy.
comments.explanation: This claim is mathematically inaccurate. Deformable convolution aggregates values using learned offsets with fixed or uniform weights. In contrast, even with $K=1$, the deformable attention module still computes query-dependent attention weights and applies value projections—operations absent in deformable convolutions. The two do not share the same functional form. The module only approximates deformable convolution if attention weights are uniform and value transformations are identity, which is not enforced. The authors should rephrase to say that the module becomes a highly sparse attention pattern with limited adaptability, rather than claiming equivalence to a different operation.

comments.title: Inconsistent FLOPs comparison with DETR-DC5
comments.quote: Our proposed Deformable DETR has on par FLOPs with Faster R-CNN + FPN and DETR-DC5. But the runtime speed is much faster  $(1.6\times)$  than DETR-DC5, and is just  $25\%$  slower than Faster R-CNN + FPN.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'on par FLOPs' contradicts Table 1: Deformable DETR has 173G FLOPs vs. 187G for DETR-DC5—a 7.5% reduction, not parity. While the 1.6× speedup (19 vs. 12 FPS) is real, it suggests memory access efficiency dominates runtime. However, the paper does not profile or explain this gap. The inconsistency undermines the efficiency narrative. Rewrite to reflect actual values: 'Deformable DETR reduces FLOPs by 7.5% and achieves 1.6× faster inference, indicating memory access patterns play a key role.'

comments.title: AP_S improvement conflates architectural and training changes
comments.quote: DETR, Deformable DETR achieves better performance (especially on small objects) with  $10 \times$  less training ep
comments.explanation: The claim attributes improved $AP_S$ (26.4 vs. 20.5) to deformable attention, but Deformable DETR also uses Focal Loss and 300 queries—changes not present in the compared DETR baseline. The $DETR-DC5^+$ row (36.2 AP, 16.3 $AP_S$ at 50 epochs) shows that training improvements alone yield gains, but the ablation does not isolate the effect of deformable attention. Without controlling for these factors, the attribution is premature. The authors should clarify that the improvement combines multiple design choices and provide ablation to isolate the contribution of deformable attention to small object detection.

comments.title: Ambiguous FPN usage in ablation table
comments.explanation: The 'FPNs' column is inconsistently labeled: some rows specify FPN/BiFPN, others say 'w/o', and some are blank. It is unclear whether blank entries mean no FPN or an unspecified one. For example, the last row (AP=43.8) matches the first row (which uses FPN) but has a blank FPNs field—suggesting ambiguity. Since FPN affects feature alignment, the model's reliance on it must be clearly stated. All entries should be filled to indicate exactly which pyramid (if any) is used, and 'w/o' should be defined as 'without FPN' for clarity.

comments.title: Missing efficiency metrics in state-of-the-art comparison
comments.quote: Table 3 compares the proposed method with other state-of-the-art methods. Iterative bounding box refinement and two-stage mechanism are both utilized by our models in Table 3. With ResNet-101 and ResNeXt-101 (Xie et al., 2017), our method achieves 48.7 AP and 49.0 AP without bells and whistles, respectively. By using ResNeXt-101 with DCN (Zhu et al., 2019b), the accuracy rises to 50.1 AP. With additional test-time augmentations, the proposed method achieves 52.3 AP.
comments.explanation: Table 3 reports only accuracy metrics, omitting any efficiency measures like FLOPs, inference time, or memory usage—despite the paper's emphasis on computational efficiency. For fair comparison with methods like EfficientDet-D7, which are designed for speed, runtime or cost must be reported. The absence undermines the claim that Deformable DETR is a more practical end-to-end detector. The authors should add a column for FLOPs or inference speed (ms) in Table 3 or reference a supplementary table with these metrics.

comments.title: Incorrect complexity expression for deformable attention
comments.quote: the complexity of the deformable attention module is of  $O(2N_{q}C^{2} + \min (HWC^{2},N_{q}KC^{2}))$  (See Appendix A.1 for details). When it is applied in DETR encoder, where  $N_{q} = HW$ , the complexity becomes  $O(HWC^{2})$ , which is of linear complexity with the spatial size.
comments.explanation: The stated complexity $O(2N_qC^2 + \min(HWC^2, N_qKC^2))$ is misleading. The dominant cost comes from computing offsets and weights ($O(N_q \cdot 3MKC)$) and bilinear sampling ($O(N_q M K C)$), not $C^2$ terms. When $N_q = HW$, the correct complexity is $O(HW \cdot M K C)$, which is linear in spatial size only if $M, K, C$ are fixed. The current form suggests a $C^2$ bottleneck that does not reflect actual computation. The expression should be rewritten as $O(N_q M K C)$ to accurately reflect the linear dependence on spatial size and constant $M, K, C$.

comments.title: Typo in funding source terminology
comments.quote: the National Natural Science Foundation of China under grand No.U19B2044 and No.61836011.
comments.explanation: The word 'grand' is a clear typo for 'grant'. This affects professionalism in a formal acknowledgment section. Given that this lists major funding sources, precision in terminology is important. Correct 'grand' to 'grant' to maintain credibility and clarity.

comments.title: Incorrect singular verb for plural subject
comments.quote: At the core of Deformable DETR are the (multi-scale) deformable attention modules, which is an efficient attention mechanism in processing image feature maps.
comments.explanation: The relative clause uses 'which is' with a plural antecedent 'modules'. This violates subject-verb agreement. The correct form is 'which are' to match the plural subject. Alternatively, rephrase to 'which together form an efficient attention mechanism' for better clarity. The current version reads as grammatically incorrect and should be corrected.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents Deformable DETR, which replaces the standard Transformer attention with a deformable attention module that samples only a small set of key points, aiming to address DETR's slow convergence and poor small-object detection. While the core idea is sound and the experimental results are strong, several issues weaken the paper's claims and completeness.

The paper makes a meaningful contribution by introducing deformable attention for end-to-end detection, achieving faster convergence and better small-object performance. The experimental results on COCO are compelling and the code release supports reproducibility. However, the paper lacks a clean ablation isolating the effect of deformable attention from other changes (e.g., focal loss, more queries), does not report computational cost (FLOPs, memory, FPS) for the two-stage variant, and does not discuss failure cases or limitations of the sparse sampling approach.

Overview issues:

- Ablation does not isolate the effect of deformable attention: The main comparison in Table 1 (Deformable DETR vs. DETR-DC5^+) changes multiple factors simultaneously: deformable attention, focal loss, number of object queries (100→300), and training schedule (500→50 epochs). The DETR-DC5^+ baseline incorporates focal loss and 300 queries but still uses standard attention. The paper does not include a DETR-DC5^+ variant trained for 500 epochs to see if the gap persists, nor does it ablate the query count or loss function. Without a controlled experiment where only the attention mechanism changes, the claimed 10× speedup cannot be attributed solely to deformable attention. The authors should add a DETR-DC5^+ with 300 queries and focal loss trained for 500 epochs, or train Deformable DETR for 500 epochs to show convergence behavior.

- Computational cost not fully reported for all variants: Table 1 reports FLOPs and FPS for the base Deformable DETR but does not provide these metrics for the iterative refinement and two-stage variants. The two-stage variant uses an encoder-only proposal network that processes all pixels, which likely increases computation. The paper claims efficiency but does not verify it for the best-performing models. The authors should report FLOPs, parameter count, and inference FPS for all variants in Table 1, and ideally also for the state-of-the-art comparisons in Table 3.
